title: Act I–II: The Hypocrite Installed
paragraphs: CHARACTERS: MADAME PERNELLE, Orgon's mother. ORGON, a wealthy bourgeois. ELMIRE, Orgon's wife. DAMIS, Orgon's son. MARIANE, Orgon's daughter. VALÈRE, in love with Mariane. CLÉANTE, Orgon's brother-in-law. TARTUFFE, a hypocrite. DORINE, Mariane's maid. MONSIEUR LOYAL, a bailiff. A POLICE OFFICER. FLIPOTE, Madame Pernelle's maid.
ACT I, SCENE I
(The house of ORGON, Paris. MADAME PERNELLE is on her way out, with ELMIRE, MARIANE, CLÉANTE, DAMIS, and DORINE.)
MADAME PERNELLE: Come, come, Flipote; it's time I left this place. I can't keep up with you; I walk at my own pace.
ELMIRE: You walk so fast we can hardly— Mother, wait!
MADAME PERNELLE: Enough! No more! Stay here, my son's wife. I can't abide the nonsense and the strife.
ELMIRE: We only tried to— Mother—
MADAME PERNELLE: You run a very sloppy household here. Dorine, your mistress acts just as I feared. In my day, servants didn't answer back to every whim. They knew how to be tact.
DORINE: (aside) If—
MADAME PERNELLE: And you, my dear, you've always had this way of putting in your word at every turn, each day.
DAMIS: But—
MADAME PERNELLE: You're a fool, boy. Plain and simple. How your father bears it is a wonder to us all. Where Tartuffe is concerned—
CLÉANTE: But surely—
MADAME PERNELLE: You, sir, are her brother, I respect that, but I don't respect your views on God and man.
MARIANE: But mother—
MADAME PERNELLE: And you, child, are too mild, too meek, too sweet. In this house Tartuffe is the man complete, and everyone should follow what he says. He is a saint who shows us better ways.
DORINE: A saint! He came in here a beggar from the street, and now he bosses everyone we meet!
MADAME PERNELLE: His rank was low. That only proves his worth. The truly humble rise above the earth.
DORINE: And that's Tartuffe?
MADAME PERNELLE: Yes. That is Tartuffe.
(She sweeps out. CLÉANTE turns to DORINE.)
CLÉANTE: What manner of man is this Tartuffe exactly?

DORINE: A man who prays loudly in public and eats well in private. A man who came to this house starving, and now directs it. He tells Orgon what to think, what to eat, how to pray. And Orgon—(She shakes her head.)—Orgon believes every word.
ACT I, SCENE V
(ORGON returns from the country. He speaks to DORINE.)
ORGON: Ah! Good day, all. What's the news at home? How does the family? Is everyone in health?
DORINE: Your wife had a fever two days back and felt quite ill—
ORGON: And Tartuffe?
DORINE: Tartuffe? Oh, Tartuffe is quite well. Rosy and fat, and full of good cheer—
ORGON: Poor man.
DORINE: That night your wife was unable to sup—her head ached so—
ORGON: And Tartuffe?
DORINE: He supped alone and ate with good appetite—two partridges, and half a leg of mutton.
ORGON: Poor fellow.
DORINE: (staring) Sir—your wife—
ORGON: Yes, yes, I'll go and see her. (But he doesn't move.) And Tartuffe—did he sleep well?
DORINE: (giving up) Like a top, sir. Like a top.
ORGON: Poor man. He works so hard at virtue.

title: Act III: The Seduction
paragraphs: ACT III
(ELMIRE and TARTUFFE, alone. DAMIS has hidden himself to observe.)
TARTUFFE: (taking ELMIRE's hand) Madam, I would speak with you—my heart is full—
ELMIRE: (pulling away) Indeed, Tartuffe?

TARTUFFE: Do not be alarmed. I am—I confess it—not entirely the man I appear. No saint is made of stone. I am devout, but I am also flesh, and your beauty—your remarkable, sinful beauty—has done its work on me. I love you, madam. I love you with a burning, pious love.
ELMIRE: (coolly) That is a most unusual declaration.

TARTUFFE: Let it be unusual, so long as it is honest. I have prayed against this feeling. I have fasted. I have afflicted my body. But God—(He raises his eyes piously.)—God sees fit to test me with this passion.
ELMIRE: Does He indeed. And my husband—your devoted friend—
TARTUFFE: Orgon sees things as he chooses to see them. A man so devout sometimes forgets to look at what is in front of him. Whereas I see you clearly, madam.
ELMIRE: Monsieur Tartuffe—

TARTUFFE: If you would speak of this to your husband—I beg you, do not. Not because I fear exposure—God forgives sinners who repent—but because the shock might harm him. He has such faith in me. It would destroy him.
(ELMIRE looks at him for a long moment, then nods slightly.)
ELMIRE: I will say nothing—for now. But this—conversation—must not be repeated.
TARTUFFE: (taking this as encouragement) Your mercy overwhelms me. Your charity is saintly.
(DAMIS bursts from hiding.)
DAMIS: Ha! I heard everything! Father will hear of this—
(ORGON enters at the sound of commotion.)
DAMIS: Father! Tartuffe has just made love to your wife!
ORGON: (looking at TARTUFFE) Is this true?
TARTUFFE: (falling to his knees) Yes. It is true. I am a sinner, a wretch, a vile creature unworthy of your trust. Dismiss me, master. Drive me out. I deserve it.
ORGON: (to DAMIS) You villain. To slander this holy man—
DAMIS: Father, I heard—
ORGON: Get out of my sight! (To TARTUFFE.) Rise, my friend. My son lies. I know your heart.
TARTUFFE: No, master, he speaks truth—
ORGON: He lies! And you—your humility proves your holiness. (He leads TARTUFFE away.) Come, let us leave this den of slander.
(ELMIRE is left standing alone.)
ELMIRE: (to herself) What is Orgon made of, that he cannot see?

title: Act IV–V: Exposure and Justice
paragraphs: ACT IV
(ORGON has now given Tartuffe the deed to his house and made him sole beneficiary of his estate. ELMIRE devises a plan.)
ELMIRE: Orgon, I want to show you something. Will you hide under this table and observe?
ORGON: Hide? Under a table?
ELMIRE: Yes. I will speak to Tartuffe—and you will hear what kind of man he is. Will you trust your own ears?
ORGON: (reluctantly) I trust Tartuffe. But—very well. To prove it, I will hear him. (He hides under the table.)
(TARTUFFE enters.)
TARTUFFE: You sent for me?

ELMIRE: Yes. (With calculated hesitation.) You spoke—yesterday—of your feelings. I have thought about what you said. I—I am not unmoved. But you must show me—demonstrate—that your feelings are genuine.
TARTUFFE: (immediately emboldened) Madam—my feelings are the sincerest—
ELMIRE: And my husband—what of him?
TARTUFFE: (waving his hand) Orgon is easily managed. A man so pious is always a little blind. What the eye does not see—(He moves closer.) —the heart does not grieve for. Between ourselves, madam—
(A loud crash from under the table. ORGON emerges.)
ORGON: (white-faced) I have heard enough.
TARTUFFE: (not missing a beat) You have heard a test of your wife's virtue, which she has passed admirably—
ORGON: Get out of my house.
TARTUFFE: (coldly) Your house, is it? I think you will find—if you review the documents you signed—that this house is rather mine.
ORGON: What?
TARTUFFE: The deed you signed over to me—the estate—the property—all quite legal, quite binding. Good day, Orgon. I shall return with the proper authorities.
(He goes.)
ORGON: (sitting down heavily) What have I done.
ACT V
(MONSIEUR LOYAL appears with officers to evict the family. Then a POLICE OFFICER appears.)
POLICE OFFICER: (to TARTUFFE) You, sir. You are under arrest.
TARTUFFE: What?

POLICE OFFICER: By order of His Majesty the King. Your true identity is known. You are wanted in connection with crimes in several provinces. The documents you hold are null and void; the estate is restored to its rightful owner.
(TARTUFFE is led away.)
ORGON: (stunned) How—
CLÉANTE: The King sees all, brother. The truly devout are protected; the hypocrites are found out. It is the moral of the story.
DORINE: (drily) So the King solved everything. Convenient.
CLÉANTE: (to ORGON) And you have learned something?
ORGON: (ruefully) I have learned that a man who prays very loudly in public is worth watching.
DORINE: That is a start.
CLÉANTE: And that virtue is not the same as the appearance of virtue.
ORGON: Yes. Yes, I have learned that too. (He looks around at his family.) Forgive me. All of you.
MARIANE: (taking his hand) Father.
(The family is reunited. VALÈRE and MARIANE will be married. Order is restored.)
CURTAIN.
